results.possibleIdentity: Phillip B. DeNee was a physicist and biophysicist whose career spanned research in solid-state physics, materials science, and occupational health. He studied physics at Lehigh University, where he was a member of the physics department in the late 1950s and graduated around 1960. Following his education, he was appointed to the staff of the Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) in Tennessee in the early 1960s. By the late 1960s, DeNee was employed by the American Machine and Foundry (AMF) at their research center in Stamford, Connecticut. During this time, he focused on fields such as solid-state physics, magnetic materials, and electronics. His work in the New York-Connecticut engineering corridor led to his association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that promoted collaborations between the technical and artistic communities. By the early 1970s, DeNee transitioned into biophysics and occupational health, joining the Pittsburgh Mining and Safety Research Center of the U.S. Bureau of Mines and later the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) in Morgantown, West Virginia. He became a specialist in scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and backscattered electron imaging, publishing extensively on the characterization of respirable mine dust, coal particles, and the health effects of fibrous glass exposure.
